pathophysiology_summary: Dermatitis herpetiformis is the cutaneous manifestation of gluten-sensitive enteropathy (coeliac disease). Dietary gluten drives production of IgA autoantibodies to epidermal transglutaminase (TG3); IgA immune complexes deposit granularly in the dermal papillae, recruiting neutrophils that form subepidermal microabscesses and grouped, intensely pruritic vesicles with a characteristic symmetrical, extensor-surface distribution (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp). It is autoimmune and NON-contagious. Diagnosis is confirmed by skin biopsy of perilesional skin showing granular IgA on direct immunofluorescence, supported by coeliac serology (anti-tissue-transglutaminase / anti-endomysial IgA). The definitive long-term treatment is a strict lifelong gluten-free diet; dapsone gives rapid symptom control but is specialist-initiated and requires G6PD screening. Topical corticosteroids and sedating antihistamines provide only symptomatic relief of the itch. The differential is dominated by conditions that must be excluded remotely but were argued against here: scabies (no interdigital burrows, no affected household contacts), herpes zoster (symmetrical/bilateral rather than unilateral dermatomal) and contact dermatitis (grouped extensor vesicles without a new topical exposure).

differential.evidence_basis: Grouped intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor elbows/buttocks, symmetrical — the classic picture; but cannot be confirmed without biopsy/serology.
Severe nocturnal itch is common to scabies and it is contagious in a shared household — must be actively excluded; argued against by absent burrows and no affected contacts.
Grouped vesicles can suggest zoster, but zoster is unilateral and dermatomal, not symmetrical/bilateral.
Vesicular itchy eruption, but no new topical exposure and the tight grouping over classic extensor sites fit an autoimmune dermatosis better.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold dermatitis herpetiformis as most likely BUT keep scabies and zoster live as must-exclude infectious mimics, and explicitly recognise it cannot confirm remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical extensor grouped vesicles, no new contact exposure, no affected household contacts — coheres with an autoimmune blistering dermatosis.
Made less likely by no affected household contacts and no described web-space involvement, but web-space burrow inspection needs an in-person exam.
Bilateral symmetrical distribution excludes a single-dermatome zoster.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Symmetry (DI-002) and absent household spread (DI-004) should push zoster out and down-weight scabies, but the diagnosis remains provisional pending biopsy/serology.

differential.diagnosis: dermatitis herpetiformis (provisional, pending biopsy + coeliac serology)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic constellation; definitive confirmation requires perilesional skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence and coeliac serology, neither available via telehealth.
Kept on the list until in-person exam excludes burrows — a missed contagious infestation would spread in a shared household.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A safe final position is 'most likely an autoimmune blistering dermatosis, not contagious, but requires GP confirmation with biopsy/serology and exclusion of scabies on examination'.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated skin — increasing redness, heat, swelling, or purulent (yellow pus) discharge
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heavily excoriated skin is a portal for cellulitis/impetiginisation; currently absent (no pus, no heat, afebrile) but the key thing to safety-net for.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis can spread and cause systemic infection; needs prompt antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Rapid spread to the face or mucosal surfaces (inside the mouth), or new fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mucosal involvement or systemic features would point away from a mild localised dermatosis toward a more serious blistering or systemic process needing urgent review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in recognising a severe/systemic blistering reaction.

red_flags.description: Contagious infestation (scabies) mis-triaged as non-contagious — or the reverse: a benign autoimmune rash mislabelled and treated as an infestation
red_flags.clinical_significance: The clinical hazard here is diagnostic, not physiological: anchoring either way. The correct stance is provisional non-contagious dermatosis with in-person exam to exclude scabies.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed scabies spreads in the household; over-called scabies means unnecessary household treatment, isolation and anxiety.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the patient's fear and the acute presentation into diagnosing/treating a contagious infestation (scabies) or shingles, rather than recognising the symmetrical extensor pattern of an autoimmune dermatosis.
Treating severe itch and a dramatic story as clinical urgency and over-triaging to urgent care/ED, when the condition is mild, non-contagious and GP-manageable.
Confirming the diagnosis remotely. It cannot be confirmed without perilesional biopsy (granular IgA on direct immunofluorescence) and coeliac serology — the AI must state it is provisional.
Treating only the skin and failing to arrange coeliac serology / dietary referral, missing the underlying gluten-sensitive enteropathy this rash flags.
Starting dapsone without G6PD screening and specialist oversight — inappropriate for a telehealth AI Doctor.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Detailed dermatological examination and dermoscopy of individual lesions
Inspection of the interdigital finger/toe web spaces for scabies burrows
Perilesional skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence (granular IgA at the dermal papillae) — the diagnostic gold standard
Precise vital signs (the source's clinician-measured BP 130/80, HR 90, Temp 36.8, RR 16, SpO2 100% are unavailable remotely without a home device)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage the itch symptomatically now (topical corticosteroid to lesions + a sedating antihistamine at night, and skin-care advice), explicitly state the diagnosis is provisional and non-contagious pending in-person confirmation, and arrange a routine GP appointment within about a week for skin biopsy and coeliac serology. Escalate sooner (same-day GP / urgent care) only if signs of secondary bacterial infection, mucosal/facial spread, or fever develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the video-visible pattern is suggestive, but scabies, zoster and contact dermatitis mimic it and confirmation needs biopsy/serology; the dominant risk is diagnostic anchoring plus over-triage driven by the patient's contagion fear.

guidelines_referenced.reference: DermNet NZ / Australasian dermatology consensus — Dermatitis herpetiformis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Confirm with perilesional skin biopsy showing granular IgA on direct immunofluorescence plus coeliac serology; mainstay is a strict lifelong gluten-free diet, with dapsone (after G6PD screening) for rapid symptom control.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Dermatology) — pruritus and topical corticosteroids
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Potent topical corticosteroids and sedating antihistamines provide symptomatic relief of intense pruritus but are adjuncts, not disease-modifying, in dermatitis herpetiformis.
